speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Thursday, July 6, 1995. The survivor is Jean Greenstein. The interviewer is Mary Rothschild. City/State, Los Angeles, California, United States of America. And the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am Mary Rothschild interviewing Jean Greenstein for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. It's July 6, 1995. The interview will be conducted in English. This is July 6, 1995. I'm interviewing Jean Greenstein for the Survivors of the Shoah Foundation. Tape one. Please give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jean, J-E-A-N. That's my first name. My last name is Greenstein, G-R-E-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My name was Jean-- G-R-U-N-S-T-E-I-N, Grunstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you're born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the city by the name Velky Sevlus, then Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember many, many things. Most is the place I was born in. Of course, because my grandmother and grandfather lived there also. And I also remember at the age of four, my father was an officer in the Austro-Hungarian army, Austro-Hungarian army. And he kept a gun from World War I in his night table. And one of the people that worked for us asked me to take it out and show it to him. And I did when I was four years old. And he picked up the gun and shot me right through the neck. And I wound up in the hospital for about six months. I was not able to talk for about a year or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that an accident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then I also remember having a younger brother who died, I believe of scarlet fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names? My father's name was, in, in Jewish it was Pinkus. And then in, in Czech at the time was Peter. And my mother's name was Sarah. And I think that was in every name. Sari it was in Hungarian. What do you remember about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember they were devoted parents-- devoted to their children. And also devoted to their family. Both my grandparents were still alive at the time. And I remember visiting, almost on a daily basis, on and off my grandparents from my mother's side and from my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived also in the same city of Velky Sevlus, but they were in different directions. And that was no big problem, because the city itself was not such a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my maternal grandfather was a farmer and also an artist. He did paintings. He did wood carving. He also made violins. I remember that very well. And he played the violin. I remember him being a religious individual. Quite religious. And also, he had a small farm in his backyard after he retired. He used to be, actually, a, a, a manager of a farm-- of an estate, actually. But then after he retired, he lived in the city and he had a small farm. And he tilled the land and had fruit trees and vegetable gardens that we used for the family and also for sale.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Roth. And my paternal grandfather was also a manager in a farm. And he also lived in the same city after he retired. Mind you, the farms were just about 1 or 2 kilometers from our hometown. So, after they retired, they both moved inside-- within the city limits. And he also did small farming. And he had four sons. And one of them-- the oldest one was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a dentist. And he practiced dentistry all my life that I know of. I think he got his degree in the year that I was born. And practiced-- and he did financially quite well and supported both sets of grandparents and probably most of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lived in a-- quite a large home. What it was considered then an affluent home. And my father was prominent in the city, well known. And he was known as a quality dentist. And had patients from all over. Not only from my hometown, but from other cities coming for services.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides myself, five more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were quite close. Except for my oldest sister, who was away in school. And actually she came back pretty close to the time when the family was broken apart. So, I think for about eight years, she was in boarding school in a different town, so I had no close connection. But with the other four members of my immediate family, we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very pretty lady. I remember that. With auburn, sort of chestnut, reddish hair. Chestnut brown or chestnut, reddish hair. Tall. And a very good mother and devoted to her children. Very devoted to her children and her own family. So we were sort of a close-knit family. And, and I remember many occasions about my mother, also my father. We had a chance because, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bad vibes, bad messages were coming through from Germany. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such as the Nazis were coming to power. I remember that. And we had chances to move away from my hometown. And for a few reasons, they didn't want to leave their family behind. And also my father would need schooling all over again to practice in his profession something that he was automatically doing at home. And, of course, nobody believed that what was coming will ever happen. And, therefore, they thought about it, talked about it, but never really seriously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were observant but not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure I remember holidays. We used to go to temple on holidays. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mainly, you know, the High Holidays and also Pesach holiday. We used to go-- we had-- We observed the Kosher home, but not, not the Orthodox. We were not-- especially in our hometown, we were far from being Orthodox. My father did not work Saturdays. But he did work Sundays. And that was for two reasons. One was a religious one. And the other one was it was more convenient for people who observed the Saturday to come as patients on Sundays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke Hungarian, mainly Hungarian. Of course, I went to school, Czech school, and I learned Czech. And, and also, I used to go to my uncle in Pressburg, Bratislava. And I stayed there summers, and I learned how to speak German. Especially at home, although my grandparents did speak Yiddish. But at home, we did not speak Yiddish. So I had no chance to learn the Yiddish language. So I wound up learning German in Bratislava, or Pressburg, whatever you may call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember public school. Being taken by one of the nurses of my father, because that's who I felt more comfortable to go there. Of course, I remember also kindergarten. I was taken to kindergarten. And I remember enjoying myself. I think I had about two years of kindergarten. And actually, one year. Because when I was four, if you remember, I told you before I was shot in the neck. And for about six months, I was in the hospital and rehabilitation and all things like that. Then, I remember being either a year or year and a half in kindergarten. And then public school. I think the first year of public school I spent in sort of a Hebrew school. But I did not like it. So I was transferred into regular public school. And it was a Czech school at the time, so I learned Czech well. And I spoke the language, and I was quite proficient in it. Until the year 1939, when Czechoslovakia fell apart and we became occupied under Hungarian regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939, what were your favorite subjects in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always engineering and chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you wanted to be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I wanted to do something in engineering. No question about it. Didn't want to be in dentistry like my father. Although I spent a lot of time-- we had-- my father had a dental practice. But those days we had our own laboratory, and we had like two or three technicians working there. And I spent a lot of time with them. And, therefore, I learned the trade from the bottom up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents of antisemitism that happened to you personally before 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in this school where I believe I was a good student. And I remember once a kid, who was not Jewish, came up to me to help him in some of the homework in math. And I told him I can't do it now, but I will do it later. And he and two of his friends started beating me up. You Jew bastard. You don't want to share your knowledge with anybody. And they really beat me up pretty badly. I went in front of the-- at that time, don't remember-- if you remember, it was Czechoslovakia yet. And I went in front of the teacher or the principal. I honestly don't remember. And complained to him. And, of course, he took it, but not very seriously. He says, oh, that's what children do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to be 11, 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was 1934? 1935?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like '34, '35, yes. And I had a couple incidents after that in higher learning. Because in our hometown, there were many ethnic Germans. And, don't forget, with the-- rise of Hitler in Germany prompted them to be more antisemitic than they were previously. So there many small incidents took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- excuse me. I remember them telling me that something that really happened. That a holocaust is going to take place. They are going to hang every Jew just for being Jewish. And I should turn my religion right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, you know-- I don't believe I was 15 or 16. And I wanted to ask them, supposing if I turn religion. Will that make me a better person just because I turn? Oh, yeah, you'll be saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these incidents that happened many, many times. That, especially during the Easter time. When they used to come home from church, and the next day we came. And, of course, the priests used to edge them on that we killed Jesus Christ. And I remember the arguments. You know, I really don't remember that far back. And I reassured them that I had nothing to do with it. I don't think it went over very well. But that's what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hometown was a city of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 4,000 or 5,000 Jews out of 14,000 or 16,000. I don't-- that's debatable. Some people say it's 14,000, and some people say it's 16,000. Whatever it is, we had about 4,000 or 5,000 Jews. We had also a segment of Germans and a segment of Czechs, and also a segment of Hungarians and also Russians. At a certain part of the city, there were, I would say 1,000 or 2,000 families who were of Russian origin. This is Slavic people of the Eastern Orthodox faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in the Jewish Quarter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived on the main street. Well, there were many Jewish people there, because the Jews, as you know, in Europe were a merchant class. And very often, they would have their business in the main street, and they would live in back of their business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, many of our neighbors were Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And many of my friends were Jewish. And also, many of my friends were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were German. As a matter of fact, one of my best friends was an ethnic German. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Who I went to school with, all the way through until I was 19 years old. Then he was called up to the German army. And they automatically took him into the SS. I don't know whether he survived the war or not. I have no idea. Never heard from him since. But also I had many Jewish friends. And I used to play soccer with them, used to go to the park. The only exception that I had is while I was in Czechoslovakia in the summers, I used to go to Bratislava to my uncle's. Stayed with him. And, and I guess my father had one purpose, for me to learn German well. Because he himself spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I speak it still today, quite well, as a matter of fact. Came handy later in life. And I'll tell you that later, if you ask me the question.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the first signs that there was trouble for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, already in 1939, when I started-- well actually, I was a year into my engineering school, many of the-- especially the ethnic Germans and Hungarians-- were telling me what do you need school for? They're going to hang you anyway, so-- they're going to kill you anyway. This was already in 1939. It was still Czechoslovakia. And, of course, I didn't believe it. But in March 15, of 1939, that really happened. The Hungarians marched in with some Germans, and they dismembered Czechoslovakia as we knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, not too much, really. Because my father was an officer in the Austro-Hungarian army. He was quite a decorated one. And the ethnic Hungarians came to him and talked to him, you know, sort of, oh-- don't forget, the Hungarian troops marched in. And the Hungarians looked at them as liberators. I looked at them as occupiers. And the Czech army was a very modern, updated version at that time-- probably the most modern army in Europe. And the Hungarians brought in a rag-tag army that looked something like from a relic of World War I. And I remember quite a few Hungarian and German people of the community, who were very antisemitic, greeted them and, and sort of encouraging them. We have many Jews that you have to take care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were then 15 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very good. It felt very uncomfortable. I sort of was born in Czechoslovakia of Jewish faith, but I-- my loyalty was to the state. No matter what the state would have been. But so happened that I was a Czechoslovakian. My loyalty was to them. And-- Although, my father didn't feel the same way, because he was born in the Austro-Hungarian monarchy, in the same city. But there was a-- so he had memories, and especially that he was an officer in the Austro-Hungarian army. It meant something to him. To me, it didn't mean anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yes, I felt that I lost something right then and there. Not only security, but also something that I was-- I grew up with and something that I had loyalty to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially felt uneasy about the threats that these classmates of mine made against another classmate. And don't forget, I used to play soccer. And we were very friendly. I used to go to their home. And then when-- I think around 1937, it started strongly that these kids said, we don't want Jews in my home. And my mother doesn't. My father doesn't want you to come anymore. And he doesn't want me to go to your house. But I will go to your house-- the children. But then, in 1939, everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed that they really-- we were sort of ostracized in the classes. The non-Jews on one side, and the Jews on the other side. Except this one individual, Schlier, who was a classmate of mine who used to come to my home. We used to do our homework together. And we were one of the better students of the whole class.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I finished school. And I got sort of a junior engineering, it was called at that time. Of course, I couldn't-- as a Jew, I couldn't get a job. Because most of the jobs were going to defense industry. And I was not trusted. So I stayed home, and I worked with my father and learned to be a dental technician. And, by the way, my father's license was revoked. Because at that time, they wanted a non-Jew to own the business. They outlawed Jews in 1943, I believe it was-- end of '43. They outlawed Jews having businesses. And, of course, even as a professional, my father had a license from the city or the state, and they revoked that. However, there was no other dentist who was not Jewish. So they allowed him to practice. But, immediately, there were tremendous tensions already because the ethnic Germans were taken into the SS. And they came home visiting and claiming how many Jews they murdered. And quite bragging about it. Of course, nobody believed it. But I did. I did believe it. Of course, then we approaching of 1944, I'm sure you know that the Hungarian Jews were molested, but not too much. Until 1944, I believe, March the 15th or March something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your life between 1939 and 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say very uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, at that time until the end of 1943-- actually June of 1943, I was going to school. And my classmates who were non-Jews, except-- exception of one or two, were very, very antisemitic. and continuously telling me, what are you studying for? As I mentioned before. They're going to kill you anyway. Why do you have to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have to bother even? And I said, well, I still like to have a profession because somehow, I will-- I will-- hoping to survive. And I'm building my own future. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then in 1943 in June, I finished school. And as I told you before, I could not get a job in the defense industry. Because everything was defense. So I worked with my father and I learned to be a dental technician. Until, 19-- I believe, March 1944, the government of Hungary was overthrown and a new government was appointed. Quisling-- I don't remember his name. And we were overran by German SS troopers and Hungarian gendarmes. And I remember, it was very frightening. Because the Jews were forbidden to go the streets after dark. They were obliged to wear a yellow armband with a Magen David. And our movements were totally restricted. We were not allowed to go to school anymore. Although, that didn't somehow involve me, personally, but my brothers and my sisters, it did. They were not allowed to go to school anymore. They had to sit in the house. We were allowed to go out only a certain hour of the day to the market, to buy food, or something like that. But anyway, they already were beating up people. If they were Jewish, if they caught them, for no reason at all they would just beat them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, but later on, yes. When they started collecting the Jews into the ghetto, that's when I was beaten up very badly by somebody that I knew. That was the amazing thing about it. And the reason for that is because I had a girlfriend who was not Jewish. And she was my father's dental assistant. And her father was a top Nazi in our hometown. He was actually, she was actually Hungarian. And her father was a member of the Hungarian Nazi party, the Arrow Cross they called them. And he was the head of the Arrow Cross. Yet, she was really, I believe, very friendly with me. Probably more than that. She was in love with me. But the father, of course-- the family forbade her to see me. And she was a pretty girl. And she had a non-Jewish boyfriend who was a year older than me. But, don't forget, I was myself only about 18 years old, and she was, like, 17. But, he knew that I used to see her. As a matter of fact, I was warned not to see her, and I didn't. But she came to see me. And he got very-- he saw us once talk together. And, I guess, he decided to take revenge on it when the Nazis closed all of the ghetto and they were collecting the Jews. I was the first one to be taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but it was sometimes in March. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the morning, a German SS man came. This guy that I told you was jealous. The SS man had a rifle. And the Hungarian had a wooden rifle. And they came for me specially. We had this big house, and we had a gate. I don't know how they broke the gate open. They came 6 o'clock in the morning. And they yell that they want me. My mother-- my mother was just hysterical, of course. They took me out, and they tied my hand in the back. And they were on bicycle. And this guy started beating me over the head, over the shoulder with this wooden rifle. And they had me run, I don't know how many kilometers at that time. And were beating me continuously. I fell once. I had to get up but I-- with my hands tied. So I was bruised very, very badly. And I got into the temple where they were collecting certain Jews that, you know-- that every non-Jew who had a gripe against anybody for anything, was--
